entry.title: Re: Budget exceeded error BP603, WBS element - PS Module
entry.content:
Standard Logic Behind Availability Control

  • Budget availability control (AVC) in PS is always checked at the project/WBS hierarchy level defined in customizing (e.g., overall project, WBS element, or statistical key figure).
  • When you allocate costs from one WBS to another within the same project, the system tries to validate against the budget profile of the receiving WBS element.
  • If the allocation standard points to another WBS in the same project, the system does not “roll up” to the project budget — it checks only the local WBS element budget.
  • That’s why you see the exceeded budget error equal to the allocation amount: the system isn’t retrieving the overall project balance, only the local WBS element’s budget.

Why It Worked Across Projects

  • When you tested with a WBS element from another project and disabled AVC (CJBW), the system didn’t enforce the same intra-project budget check.
  • Cross-project allocations are treated differently: the sending project’s AVC doesn’t apply to the receiving project’s WBS element, so the error disappears.

Is This Standard SAP Behavior?

  • Yes. SAP standard does not allow one WBS element to use another WBS element from the same project as its “budget source” in allocation standards.
  • The design assumption is that budget availability is controlled at the project or WBS level, not by cross-referencing within the same hierarchy.

Options / Workarounds

1) Change AVC Profile Level
In customizing (SPRO → Project System → Costs → Budget → Define Budget Profile), set availability control to the overall project level instead of WBS element level. This way, allocations between WBS elements within the same project will check against the total project budget, not just the receiving WBS.

2) Use Statistical WBS Elements
If you want one WBS to act as a “budget pool” for others, define it as a statistical WBS element and assign budget there. Then configure AVC to check at the project level.

3) Custom Enhancement
Some projects implement a user exit (e.g., EXIT_SAPLCJWB_001) to override how the budget is checked, so that intra-project allocations can reference another WBS’s budget. This is not standard and requires careful design to avoid audit issues.

entry.title: Service Order Template Migration – Missing Fields (Product ID / Template Search Term) and Empty API
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

I am currently migrating Service Order Templates in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and need to set the following attributes during migration:

  • Status

  • Product ID

  • Template Search Term

The migration object does not expose the fields Product ID and Template Search Term, so they cannot be maintained via the standard migration template.

As an alternative, I attempted to update these fields using the OData service API_SERVICE_ORDER_TEMPLATE_SRV. However, the API " GET" call returns an empty response, although Service Order Templates are clearly available in the system (verified in the UI).

I would appreciate clarification on the following points:

  1. Is there a supported way to populate Product ID and Template Search Term during migration?

  2. Are these fields technically supported for update via API?

  3. Under which conditions would API_SERVICE_ORDER_TEMPLATE_SRV return something

System context: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (3-system landscape, Migration Cockpit – “Migrate Your Data” app).

Best regards 

Simon

entry.title: Re: Custom logic for Payment media file
entry.content:
Hello@Dinakar1996__19

You seem to have chosen the wrong tag (Public Cloud) while posting your question.

The scenario you described is a classic payment medium exit logic (a Private Cloud/On-prem solution), which is not the supported extensibility approach in Public Cloud.

If your goal is still to “split a 60 char value into 35 + 25 in the payment file”, the public cloud offers options such as:

  • Payment format configuration (no ABAP),
  • Use a second output field that the bank format supports and,
  • Adjust the upstream data model (where you maintain the supplier or customer address in two street lines so you do not need runtime splitting).

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Day-to-day SAP Business Technology Platform operations as part of a clean core approach
entry.content:
We talk a lot about clean core.  But what happens after go-live?

Honestly, that’s where the story gets interesting.

In our webinar, “Day-to-day SAP Business Technology Platform operations as part of a clean core approach,” we’ll unpack how to confidently operate extensions on SAP Business Technology Platform, even in hybrid setups working side by side with SAP S/4HANA.

Think of it like maintaining a high-performance engine.  It’s not just about adding powerful components.  It’s about tuning, monitoring, and making sure everything runs in sync.

We’ll cover account structures, multi-tenancy strategies, delivery models, and observability.  Real-world considerations.  No theory overload.  Just what you need to keep customer landscapes stable and scalable.

If you support customers day to day, this session will resonate.  And maybe even make your next project smoother.

Join us. Let’s turn clean core into a lasting operational advantage.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/feb/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013041.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #CleanCoreStrategy #HybridCloud #SAPS4HANA #PartnerEnablement

entry.title: Re: Key User Extensibility Fields for Bill of Material no...

The Migration Object Material BOM does not support custom fields currently

 

Please check the resource Material BOM and section Custom Field Support : Not Applicable.

 

If you need to maintain custom fields have the BOM has been created then please use the API

entry.title: Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud: What Actually Gets Better (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY |  TECHNICAL GUIDE  |  SAP HANA CLOUD |  PART 1 OF 2

Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud: What Actually Gets Better

Part 1 of 2: The genuine capabilities that open up when you move from on-premise HANA to HANA Cloud — HDI containers, SAP AI Core, and the BTP application ecosystem.

By Deepa Dorairaj   | SAP AI Solution Architect  |  HANA Data Engineering  |  Published 2026

This is Part 1 of a two-part series on SAP HANA Cloud migration. Part 1 covers what genuinely improves when you move to the cloud. Part 2 covers the pain points and what to watch out for. Both perspectives are necessary for an honest picture of the migration journey.

TL;DR: Moving from on-premise HANA to HANA Cloud is not just an infrastructure change. It unlocks three capabilities that are difficult or impossible to replicate on-premise: a modern database development workflow through HDI containers and Git, access to SAP AI Core for production-grade AI deployments on your SAP data, and a rich application ecosystem through BTP that connects your HANA data to S/4HANA, Fiori, Integration Suite, and beyond.

Why This Article Exists

Most migration content falls into one of two traps: vendor-produced material that glosses over the real challenges, or war stories that focus exclusively on what broke. The honest picture is more nuanced than either.

Part 2 of this series covers the friction — the tooling gaps, scripting compatibility issues, and security model changes that will slow your team down. You should read that too, especially if you are in the planning phase.

But this article is about the other side of that equation: what genuinely gets better. Not what SAP's marketing says gets better — what actually changes for the teams doing real work in real HANA Cloud environments. The three areas covered here represent capabilities that were either unavailable, severely limited, or operationally painful in on-premise. In HANA Cloud they become first-class, production-ready patterns.

IMPROVEMENT 1 OF 3 

HDI Containers: A Modern Database Development Workflow

What HDI Actually Is — and Why It Changes Everything

The HANA Deployment Infrastructure (HDI) container model is the single biggest workflow improvement that HANA Cloud brings for development teams. On-premise HANA, most teams deploy database objects directly against schemas — DDL scripts run manually or through transport management, objects exist in a shared schema that everyone touches, and version control is an afterthought bolted on top of an inherently stateful system.

HDI flips this model entirely. Database objects are defined as source artifacts — .hdbtable, .hdbview, .hdbprocedure files — that live in a Git repository alongside application code. The HDI container is deployed from those artifacts, and the deployment engine handles dependency resolution, delta deployment, and environment isolation automatically.

The result is that your database development workflow starts to look like your application development workflow: version controlled, reviewable, deployable, and reproducible.

Git Integration: Version Control as a First-Class Citizen

On-premise HANA development rarely had true version control for database objects. Teams used SAP Transport Management for moving objects between landscapes, but transport management is not version control — it doesn't give you branching, pull requests, history, or the ability to roll back to any point in time with confidence.

HANA Cloud's HDI model integrates directly with Git repositories through SAP Business Application Studio and the HANA Cloud toolchain. Every database object is a file in a repository. Changes go through the same review process as application code. Deployments are triggered from specific commits or branches.

  • Feature branches for database changes — developers work on isolated branches without affecting the shared schema, exactly as they would for application code.
  • Pull request reviews for schema changes — DDL changes are reviewed by peers before deployment, catching errors before they reach development or production environments.
  • Full commit history for every database object — the ability to see exactly what changed, when, and who made the change, with the ability to revert to any previous state.
  • Branch-based environment promotion — development, QA, and production environments are deployed from specific branches, making the promotion process auditable and repeatable.

This is the change that development teams feel most immediately after migration. The frustration of untraceable schema changes, overwritten objects, and transport conflicts disappears when database artifacts live in Git like everything else.

Environment Isolation and Team Collaboration

On-premise HANA landscapes typically have a development system, a QA system, and a production system — three full HANA instances that are expensive to maintain and still don't provide true isolation between individual developers working simultaneously.

HDI containers provide lightweight, isolated development environments within a single HANA Cloud instance. Each developer or team can have their own container — a complete, isolated copy of the database schema — without requiring a separate HANA system. Changes in one container have zero impact on another.

  • Developer containers — each developer works in their own isolated container, testing changes without risk of interfering with colleagues.
  • Feature containers — a complete isolated environment for a specific feature or project, spun up and torn down as needed.
  • Automated dependency management — the HDI deployment engine resolves object dependencies automatically. If a view depends on a table that doesn't exist yet, the deployment fails with a clear error rather than creating an inconsistent state silently.
  • Simplified landscape transport — promoting changes from development to QA to production means deploying the same Git artifacts to different containers, not managing SAP transport requests.

Cleaner Deployment and Versioning

One of the most underappreciated HDI benefits is idempotent deployment. On-premise, re-running a DDL script against an existing object requires DROP and recreate logic, ALTER statements, or manual intervention. HDI's deployment engine compares the desired state (your artifact files) against the current state (what's in the container) and applies only the necessary changes — automatically, safely, and repeatably.

-- On-premise: manual drop and recreate required

DROP TABLE my_schema.my_table;

CREATE TABLE my_schema.my_table (id INT, name VARCHAR(100));

-- HDI: define once in .hdbtable artifact, deploy repeatedly

-- HDI engine handles delta — no manual DROP/CREATE needed

COLUMN TABLE my_table (id INT, name NVARCHAR(100));

 

Capability

What It Unlocks

Git version control

Full history, branching, pull request reviews, and rollback for every database object

Developer isolation

Individual HDI containers eliminate shared schema conflicts between developers

Automated dependency resolution

HDI deployment engine manages object dependencies — no manual sequencing of DDL scripts

Idempotent deployment

Deploy the same artifacts repeatedly without manual DROP/CREATE logic

Landscape transport

Git-based promotion replaces SAP transport requests for database objects

Feature branching

Database changes developed and tested in isolation before merging to main branch

IMPROVEMENT 2 OF 3 

SAP AI Core: Production-Grade AI on Your SAP Data

Why AI Core Changes the Enterprise AI Equation

On-premise HANA has machine learning capabilities — PAL (Predictive Analysis Library) and APL (Automated Predictive Library) provide a solid foundation for classical ML workloads that run close to the data. But these capabilities have a fundamental limitation: they are designed for traditional ML patterns and do not provide a path to deploying modern foundation models, RAG systems, or generative AI against your SAP data in a production-ready, governed way.

SAP AI Core, accessible through BTP and natively integrated with HANA Cloud, is a different category of capability entirely. It is a managed AI runtime that handles the infrastructure, scaling, and lifecycle management of AI workloads — allowing you to focus on the AI logic rather than the infrastructure that runs it.

For SAP architects and data engineers, the critical insight is this: AI Core is where your HANA Cloud data becomes the foundation for genuinely intelligent enterprise applications, not just analytical ones.

Vector Embeddings and RAG for SAP Data

HANA Cloud includes native vector storage capabilities — the ability to store and query vector embeddings directly in the database alongside your structured SAP data. This is the foundational capability for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems that can answer questions about your SAP landscape using live data rather than static training.

The architecture this enables is significant: instead of sending sensitive SAP business data to an external AI service for every query, you store embeddings of your SAP content in HANA Cloud's vector store and retrieve only the relevant context for each query. The foundation model gets the context it needs without your raw SAP data leaving your controlled environment.

-- Create a vector store table in HANA Cloud

CREATE TABLE sap_knowledge_embeddings (

  id          BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,

  content     NCLOB,

  source_type NVARCHAR(100),

  embedding   REAL_VECTOR(1536)

);

-- Semantic similarity search against SAP knowledge base

SELECT TOP 5 id, content, source_type,

  COSINE_SIMILARITY(embedding, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS score

FROM sap_knowledge_embeddings

ORDER BY score DESC;

This pattern — storing SAP process documentation, BAPI descriptions, master data definitions, and business rules as searchable embeddings — is the foundation of a genuine enterprise AI assistant that knows your specific SAP landscape, not just generic SAP knowledge.

Connecting Foundation Models via AI Core APIs

AI Core provides governed, auditable access to foundation models — including SAP's own generative AI models and third-party models through the Generative AI Hub — with the enterprise controls that on-premise environments cannot provide: usage tracking, cost allocation, rate limiting, and compliance logging built in.

The connection between HANA Cloud data and AI Core models enables patterns that were genuinely not achievable on-premise:

  • Intelligent document processing — extracting structured data from unstructured SAP documents (purchase orders, invoices, contracts) using foundation models and storing results directly in HANA Cloud tables.
  • Natural language interfaces to SAP data — allowing business users to query HANA Cloud analytics in plain language, with AI Core translating natural language to SQL and grounding responses in live data.
  • Automated exception handling — AI agents that monitor SAP business processes, identify anomalies in HANA Cloud data, and take corrective actions through SAP APIs without human intervention.
  • Fine-tuned models on SAP data — training or fine-tuning foundation models on your organization's specific SAP data patterns through AI Core's managed training infrastructure.

Grounding AI in Live SAP Business Data

The most powerful aspect of the HANA Cloud and AI Core combination is the ability to ground AI outputs in live, governed SAP business data. Generic AI models hallucinate when asked about your specific business — your customer master, your material catalog, your financial postings. Grounded models, retrieval systems, and tool-calling agents that query HANA Cloud directly produce outputs that are accurate, current, and auditable.

The architecture pattern that is emerging as the standard for enterprise SAP AI: HANA Cloud as the data foundation and vector store → AI Core as the model runtime and orchestration layer → SAP Business AI applications as the user-facing interface. Each layer does what it is best at, and the combination is genuinely more capable than any of its parts.

Capability

What It Unlocks

Native vector storage

Store and query embeddings directly in HANA Cloud alongside structured SAP data

RAG on SAP data

Build retrieval systems that answer questions using live SAP content without external data exposure

Foundation model access

Governed, auditable access to SAP and third-party foundation models through Generative AI Hub

Fine-tuning capability

Train models on your specific SAP data patterns through AI Core's managed infrastructure

Grounded AI outputs

AI responses anchored to live HANA Cloud data — accurate, current, and auditable

Intelligent automation

AI agents that monitor SAP processes and act on HANA data without human intervention

IMPROVEMENT 3 OF 3 

The BTP Application Ecosystem: Connecting HANA Cloud to Everything

From Database to Platform

On-premise HANA is primarily a database — an exceptional one, but a database. Applications connect to it, consume its data, and leave. The integration patterns are well established but fundamentally passive: HANA stores and processes data, applications request it.

HANA Cloud on BTP is part of a platform ecosystem that changes this dynamic. The connections between HANA Cloud and the broader SAP and non-SAP landscape are richer, more standardized, and more capable than what on-premise environments support. Your HANA Cloud instance is not just a database — it is a data foundation for a platform that can build, connect, and deploy enterprise applications at a speed that on-premise infrastructure cannot match.

Connecting HANA Cloud to S/4HANA and ECC

The integration between HANA Cloud and SAP's core ERP systems — S/4HANA and ECC — is one of the most practically valuable improvements for organizations running mixed landscapes. On-premise, cross-system data access required custom RFC connections, ALE configurations, or ETL pipelines that were expensive to build and fragile to maintain.

Through BTP's Integration Suite and HANA Cloud's federation capabilities, S/4HANA and ECC data becomes accessible in HANA Cloud with significantly less custom integration work. This enables analytical and AI use cases that span transactional ERP data and the broader data landscape without requiring full data replication.

  • Real-time operational reporting on S/4HANA data through HANA Cloud without impacting ERP system performance.
  • Cross-system analytics combining ERP data with data from non-SAP sources in a single HANA Cloud analytical layer.
  • AI and ML workloads that consume live ERP data through governed, auditable connections rather than stale data extracts.

Building Fiori and BTP Applications on HANA Cloud Data

SAP Business Application Studio — the cloud-based IDE that replaces the Eclipse-based tooling of on-premise development — provides a first-class development environment for building Fiori applications, CAP (Cloud Application Programming model) services, and full-stack BTP applications directly on top of HANA Cloud data.

The CAP model in particular represents a significant improvement over traditional on-premise application development patterns. CAP services expose HANA Cloud data through OData and REST APIs with built-in authorization, query handling, and SAP Fiori integration — dramatically reducing the boilerplate code required to build enterprise-grade applications on SAP data.

-- CAP service definition exposing HANA Cloud entity

service AnalyticsService {

  @readonly entity SalesOrders as

    SELECT from db.SalesOrders {

      order_id, customer, amount, status,

      created_at, region

    } where status = 'ACTIVE';

}

-- Automatically generates OData V4 endpoint

-- with filtering, sorting, and pagination built in

OData and REST API Exposure

Exposing HANA Cloud data through standardized OData and REST APIs — consumable by Fiori applications, third-party tools, mobile applications, and external systems — is significantly more streamlined on BTP than on-premise. The CAP framework handles the API layer, and BTP's API Management provides governance, rate limiting, and monitoring for those APIs across all consumers.

For organizations building an enterprise data mesh or API-first data strategy, this is one of the most compelling HANA Cloud advantages — your HANA data becomes a governed, discoverable, consumable API product rather than a database that specific applications happen to know how to query.

SAP Integration Suite and Side-by-Side Extensibility

SAP Integration Suite on BTP provides pre-built integration flows between HANA Cloud and the broader SAP and non-SAP ecosystem — Salesforce, Microsoft, ServiceNow, and hundreds of other systems — without custom middleware development. On-premise integration required custom development, dedicated middleware servers, and ongoing maintenance of bespoke integration code.

Side-by-side extensibility — the BTP pattern for extending S/4HANA and other SAP systems without modifying core — relies on HANA Cloud as its data persistence layer. Extension applications built on BTP consume S/4HANA data through governed APIs, process and store results in HANA Cloud, and surface insights back to SAP users through Fiori — all without touching the core ERP system.

Side-by-side extensibility is the pattern that makes HANA Cloud most valuable for organizations that need to innovate on top of S/4HANA without the risk and cost of core modifications. HANA Cloud is the data foundation that makes this pattern production-ready.

Capability

What It Unlocks

S/4HANA and ECC integration

Cross-system analytics and AI workloads on live ERP data without custom RFC development

CAP application development

Build Fiori and full-stack BTP applications on HANA Cloud data with dramatically less boilerplate

OData and REST APIs

Expose HANA Cloud data as governed, discoverable API products consumable by any client

SAP Integration Suite

Pre-built integration flows to SAP and non-SAP systems without custom middleware

Side-by-side extensibility

Extend S/4HANA through BTP applications backed by HANA Cloud without core modifications

BTP ecosystem

Access to the full BTP service catalog — AI, analytics, integration, automation — from a single platform

The Honest Summary

The three improvements covered in this article are not incremental upgrades to what on-premise HANA already does. They are qualitatively different capabilities that change what is possible for development teams, data engineers, and enterprise architects working with SAP data.

HDI containers with Git integration bring database development into the modern software development workflow — version controlled, isolated, reviewable, and reproducible. SAP AI Core unlocks production-grade AI on your SAP data — RAG systems, foundation model access, intelligent automation — that simply cannot be built with the same reliability and governance on-premise. And the BTP application ecosystem transforms HANA Cloud from a database into a platform foundation for the full range of SAP and cross-system enterprise applications.

None of this makes the migration easy. Part 2 of this series is honest about the friction — and there is real friction. But the capabilities waiting on the other side of that friction are substantial enough to make the journey worth taking.

Part 2 of this series covers the pain points in detail: HANA Studio to Cloud Central tooling gaps, stored procedure compatibility issues, and security model changes that will catch your team off guard. Read both before finalizing your migration plan.

entry.title: Introducing Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA)
entry.content:
As part of our ongoing commitment to continuously improve SAP Commodity Management, we are excited to introduce Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA),a next-generation product designed to meet the growing complexity and scale of modern commodity trading operations.

Commodity markets are evolving rapidly, with increasing data volumes, tighter reporting timelines, and greater demands for transparency and performance. ACRA is built to address these challenges head-on, providing a robust, future-ready foundation for commodity risk analytics.

What is ACRA?

Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA) is a comprehensive risk analytics solution that reimagines how commodity risk is calculated, processed, and reported.

At the core of ACRA is a complete redesign of the existing Risk Analytics architecture. This redesign spans, over a series of releases, the entire analytics lifecycle—from commodity position management and mark-to-market (MtM) calculations to trading profit and loss (P&L) reporting. The result is a standardized, scalable risk analytics product capable of supporting the most demanding commodity trading environments.

Our objective is simple but ambitious: to deliver a high-performance, cloud-native Risk Analytics solution that can manage extensive data volumes while simplifying adoption and lowering the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for our customers.

Why ACRA? Key Benefits

ACRA is built with both today’s requirements and tomorrow’s needs in mind. Key benefits include:

Scalability

Analytical processes are decoupled from transactional processes. As a result, they are no longer constrained by the resources of the SAP S/4HANA transactional system. These analytical processes operate as a pure cloud application and can scale naturally.

High Performance

Risk calculation is divided into simplified stacks that can be processed simultaneously in parallel threads. Persisting calculation results delivers further enhancements to reporting performance.

Future-Proof Architecture

Using modern cloud architecture and an API-first open interface design, SAP, partners, and customers can easily introduce advanced risk analytics features and custom business logic.

Seamless Integration

Native integration exists between the transactional system SAP S/4HANA and the risk analytics platform. This eliminates the need for a separate custom interface or any side-car solutions.

Delivery Flexibility

The release cycles of a cloud application are independent of the SAP S/4HANA release cycles. This speed and flexibility enable faster innovation cycles for our customers accelerating benefit realisation.

Core Features Available Today

ACRA delivers powerful functionality right out of the gate, enabling organizations to modernize their commodity risk operations immediately.

Risk Calculation Engine

  • Introduce a tailored, modular risk calculation engine for Position Reporting
  • Perform only the necessary risk calculation steps, which shortens processing time and makes the calculation workflow easier to maintain and extend
  • Enable running multiple calculation tasks at the same time to speed up processing
  • Introduce a refined integration data model for SAP S/4HANA, which reduces redundant data storage and improves consistency between transactional data and risk results
  • Executes risk calculations in the cloud (outside the SAP S/4HANA core system), which frees up core system’s resources and avoids running duplicate calculations
  • Store calculated results in the cloud as the system of record, using a two-tier storage strategy – a fast ‘hot’ layer for recent data and a lower-cost ‘cold’ layer for historical archives – to optimize performance and costs

Snapshot and Scheduling Cockpit

  • Schedule regular activities such as market data quality checks, Position calculations, end-of-day and end-of-month result validation and day-over-day comparison.
  • Provide automation of routine tasks and a clear sequence and status of the workflow – Risk analysts receive notifications when any calculation step errors out and can access error logs for diagnosis to improve control by allowing key users (e.g. risk managers) to review and approve results in a dedicated workflow before the figures are published to all users and management.

Manage Market Curves

Market Curves are centrally managed and linked to:

  • Market Products
  • Market Locations
  • Risk Domains (such as profit centres or global regions assigned to company codes)

Each Market Curve contains relevant price indexes—such as Derivative Contracts and Basis IDs—ensuring that related risk is accurately calculated and reported across the organization.

ACRA supports complex market curve definitions – you can combine multiple price indices with custom weightings or adjustments (like basis differentials) as long as their date ranges don’t overlap, instead of having to create separate curves for each scenario.

Manage Maturity Calendars

Maturity Calendars are assigned to the Derivative Contract Specification (DCS) and enable:

  • Definition of the market norm/orthodoxy
  • Early market rolls
  • Default period derivation directly from SAP S/4HANA DCS settings

This ensures consistency between trading configurations and risk analytics while providing flexibility where required.

End-of-Day (EoD) Processing

ACRA integrates with SAP’s Advanced Financial Closing (AFC) to run a robust end-of-day process that prepares consolidated position reports

This provides flexibility through:

  • Sequencing pre-delivered risk calculation steps for commodity risk calculations (Derivatives and Logistics)
  • custom processing steps to address company-specific requirements (e.g., proprietary calculations or data validation checks)
  • Include manual verification steps (for example, requiring a manager’s sign-off) to ensure accuracy before proceeding.

The result is a controlled, auditable, and flexible EoD process.

Consolidated Position End of Day Reporting

Using SAP Datasphere data models and analytical clients such as SAP Analytics Cloud, ACRA delivers comprehensive risk reporting, from high-level summaries down to individual commodity contracts and inventory positions.

Users gain full visibility into their End-of-Day positions with drill-down capabilities that support better decision-making and governance.

Conclusion - A Modern Foundation for Commodity Risk

ACRA represents a significant step forward in commodity risk analytics—combining performance, scalability, and a future-proof design into a single standardized solution. By modernizing the architecture and simplifying adoption, ACRA empowers commodity trading organizations to manage risk more effectively today while staying ready for what’s next.

Visit SAP Help Portal for more an Administration Guide: 

https://help.sap.com/docs/CCS/7b6b503b6ae0446fb3d6801c98f56b1e/349e9247507644e8a8df8a20cb60af8b.html?locale=en-US

Stay tuned for future innovations by following SAP Roadmap Explorer  for Commodity Management: 

https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=42F2E964F4E71EDAA6A3587248CD40E5&range=CURRENT-LAST

entry.title: Mass uploading Service Entry Sheets (SES) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition
entry.content:
Hi,

I’m exploring options for mass uploading Service Entry Sheets (SES) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition.

In the Public Edition, the Fiori app “Manage Service Entry Sheets – Lean Services” supports Excel uploads, which is really convenient. However, in Private Edition, I don’t see any standard Excel upload option in the app.

I’d like to know:

  1. Has anyone managed to upload SES via Excel in Private Edition?

  2. If yes, how did you implement it — standard feature, API, or custom ABAP program?

  3. Any tips, workarounds, or best practices for recurring operational uploads in Private Edition?

I could not see that Excel upload for SES in Private Edition is planned for in upcoming releases...

Any experiences, screenshots, or guidance would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks in advance,

Sara

entry.title: SAP Academic Partnership Winter Roadshow in Yerevan (Armenia)
entry.content:
SAP Academic Partnerships organised a Winter Roadshow in Yerevan with multiple SAP Events for faculties, students and partners from Yerevan State University, French University in Armenia (UFAR), Armenian State University of Economics, Yerevan Engineering City and Enterprise Incubator Foundation.

These Events included the lectures of Igor Belousov, VP of Academic Partnerships at SAP, for students and faculties with the deep dive on SAP educational programs and overview of the SAP Innovation agenda with focus on Quantum Computing and Physical AI. It also included TTT and Q&A sessions for faculties.

Students and faculties had the possibility to register (including in live regime during the Events) to several free of charge offerings such as:

  • SAP Learning Hub Student Edition: 275+ Learning Journeys with access to SAP software hands-on, academic notes for faculties and 2 certifications for each faculty and student per year
  • SAP Business Builders: gamified intro to SAP Analytics Cloud based on several business case scenarios
  • Business Builder Faculty Training: leads to obtaining of the Certificate "Business Builders Educator Accreditation"
  • SAP Signavio Academic Ediiton: business processes modelling software (REGISTER)

Special Ceremony was organised at Council Hall of Yerevan State University (YSU) to announce YSU joined SAP Academic Partnerships (University Alliances) as a Full Member. 

Vice-Rector for Scientific Affairs at YSU Rafayel Barkhudaryan noted that "within the framework of the cooperation, YSU will benefit from SAP University Alliances' educational resources, including ERP systems, data analytics, supply chain management, finance, and other areas. Free training courses will be organized for students and faculty members, and opportunities will be created to earn internationally recognized certifications."

Among the attendees at the meeting were Karlen Khachatryan, Dean of the YSU Faculty of Economics and Management, and  Karine Avetisyan, Head of the Chair of Actuarial and Financial Mathematics at the Faculty of Mathematics and Mechanics.  

Some facts about the Roadshow:

  • 150+ students
  • 50+ faculties and partners
  • 3 locations for in-person Events and 2 virtual follow-up TTT sessions

entry.title: Серія вебінарів у березні
entry.content:
Березень в SAP буде максимально насиченим подіями для тих, хто будує хмарну стратегію, працює з даними та AI, відповідає за безпеку чи підтримку користувачів. Обирайте свої must‑have вебінари та реєструйтесь за лінками нижче.

Хмара, виробництво та операції

  • 2 березня, 15:00–16:00
    Accelerate to the Cloud: SAPME/MII/PCo to SAP Digital Manufacturing
    Практичні шляхи переходу до SAP Digital Manufacturing: можливості рішень, Customer Evolution Kit, варіанти міграції та реальні кейси, що допоможуть знизити ризики й скоротити time‑to‑value.

  • 10 березня, 15:00–16:00
    SAP Cloud Application Services for Best Practices Cloud Operations
    Як керовані сервіси CAS знімають з команди рутину підтримки хмарних ландшафтів, закриваючи питання продуктивності, безпеки та інтеграцій, щоб ви фокусувались на стратегії.

  • 17 березня, 15:00–16:00
    Move Travel & Expense to the Cloud with SAP Concur
    Вебінар про перенесення процесів відряджень і витрат у хмару з SAP Concur та побудову прозорого end‑to‑end‑процесу.

Дані, аналітика та SAP Business Data Cloud

  • 5 березня, 17:00–18:00
    From Data to Decisions: Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP BDC
    Як поєднати SAP Analytics Cloud і SAP Business Data Cloud, щоб отримати єдине джерело правди для аналітики, планування та прогнозування без дублювання даних.

  • 12 березня, 17:00–18:00
    The Next Era of Business Data – From Data Silos to Business Insight: The Power of BDC Connect
    Zero‑copy підхід, інтеграція з Databricks і Snowflake, усунення data silos та побудова єдиного business data fabric для аналітики й AI.

  • 26 березня, 17:00–18:00
    The Next Era of Business Data – Design. Govern. Deliver. Data Products – Data Product Studio
    Огляд SAP Data Product Studio: моделювання, збагачення та управління data products на базі SAP Business Data Cloud з повним контролем семантики та продуктивності.

SAP BTP та AI

  • 10 березня, 17:00–18:00
    Architect the Future: SAP BTP Core Revealed
    Ключові можливості SAP Business Technology Platform: рантайми, безпека, ідентифікація, конективність та операції в гібридних і multi‑cloud сценаріях.

  • 24 березня, 15:00–16:00
    About Agents and Jewels: SAP Business AI
    Роль AI‑агентів та асистентів у стратегії SAP, практичні сценарії з SAP Business AI та взаємодія з партнерами для реалізації цінності AI в бізнес‑процесах.

  • 24 березня, 17:00–18:00
    Architect the Future: Turning Ideas into Enterprise Apps on SAP BTP
    Від коду до оркестрованих бізнес‑процесів: фреймворки, події, API та workflow‑оркестрація для швидкої розробки й інтеграції рішень на SAP BTP.

  • 31 березня, 15:00–16:00
    AI Commercial Discovery
    Огляд поточної комерційної моделі AI від SAP: безкоштовні та платні пропозиції, прогнозування споживання для підписок та кастомних агентів, моніторинг використання.

  • 14 квітня, 17:00–18:00
    Architect the Future: SAP BTP AI Best Practices – From Strategy to Implementation
    Best practices та шаблони для побудови промислових AI‑рішень на SAP BTP: як структурувати use cases, обрати підхід до реалізації та масштабувати AI з відчутним бізнес‑ефектом.

SAP Cloud ERP та оновлення продуктів

  • 12 березня, 12:00–12:30
    Continuous Innovation: Feb 2026 Updates for SAP Cloud ERP Private (MEE/EMEA)
    Огляд нового релізу SAP Cloud ERP Private та його впливу на весь ланцюг створення цінності.

  • 18 березня, 15:00–16:00
    Discover the latest Innovations in SAP Cloud ERP Private – FPS01 Release
    Інновації релізу 2025 FPS01: Joule та AI‑сценарії у продажах, логістиці, PLM, сервісі тощо, а також програми сервісів і підтримки для вашої cloud‑подорожі.

Підтримка, SAP for Me та взаємодія з SAP

  • 4 березня, 16:00–17:00
    CIC Support SAP for Me: SAP for Me Demo
    Огляд порталу SAP for Me та функції Get Support – як швидко знаходити потрібну інформацію й звертатися до підтримки.

  • 12 березня, 11:30–12:30
    CIC Support SAP for Me: Case Management
    Покрокова робота з Get Support, створення якісних кейсів і процеси SAP, яких варто дотримуватись для ефективної взаємодії з підтримкою.

  • 16 березня, 16:30–17:30
    CIC Support SAP for Me: Getting the Most from your Support
    Інфраструктура SAP Support, управління користувачами, завантаження ПЗ, ліцензії, системна адміністрація, сповіщення, сервісні запити та роль Customer Interaction Center.

  • 3 березня, 12:00–13:00
    Success Unleashed: Be future‑ready with SAP Services and Support (EMEA)
    Новий engagement‑підхід SAP Services & Support, який допоможе впевнено стартувати, зберігати імпульс змін та розкрити повну цінність інвестицій у SAP.

Безпека та відповідальне AI

  • 19 березня, 11:30–12:30 (EMEA)
    SAP Security Webcast in March: SAP’s Leadership in AI Governance with ISO 42001
    Історія отримання сертифікації ISO 42001, підходи SAP до AI‑governance на базі ISO/IEC 42001 та NIST AI RMF, вплив на сервіси Joule, SAP AI Core та SAP Business AI й підготовка до регуляцій на кшталт EU AI Act.

Стратегія даних та AI

  • 24 березня, 12:00–13:00
    The Fabric of Data & AI – Insights from leaders shaping the future of data and AI
    Дискусія з Irfan Khan та лідерами індустрії про те, як бізнес‑data fabric поєднує дані, метадані й процеси, щоб AI спирався на реальний бізнес‑контекст та приносив вимірювані результати.

Інтеграція

  • 31 березня, 17:00–18:00
    SAP Integration Suite – Monthly Update March
    Оновлення ключових можливостей SAP Integration Suite та інтерактивний блок #AskAnythingIntegration із експертами.

Щоб забронювати місце на вебінарах, переходьте за посиланнями та додавайте події до свого календаря – березень стане чудовою можливістю прокачати хмару, дані, AI та підтримку в екосистемі SAP.

entry.title: From Prompt to Production: Creating an MCP Server for Joule Studio Using AI Core
entry.content:
Introduction

Hi, I'm Max McPhee, a BTP Solution Advisor at SAP. There are a lot of new capabilities coming to the SAP Business Suite, but what excites me most is that we can now build components for AI agents entirely within BTP and host those components in BTP—making it the one-stop shop for enterprise agents.

In this blog post, I'll walk you through how I built a simple MCP (Model Context Protocol) server from scratch, using AI-powered development tools, and deployed it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry for integration with Joule Studio. The best part? The entire development process was done using conversation development with GenAI tools from SAP. 

With "MCP" literally in my name, I was obligated to dive into building an MCP server. Now let's get started.

 

What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard originally invented by Anthropic that enables AI systems to interact with external tools and data sources in a standardized way. Think of it as a universal language that allows AI agents to discover and use tools without custom integration code for each one.

SAP has been an early supporter of MCP, recognizing its potential for enterprise AI scenarios. The company has released several open-source MCP servers to the community, including:

  • UI5 MCP Server - For SAPUI5 development assistance
  • CAP MCP Server - For SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • Fiori MCP Server - For SAP Fiori development

For more details on SAP's MCP, check out SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers to Enable Agentic Development.

With Joule Studio, you can connect your own custom MCP servers to extend Joule's capabilities with tools specific to your business needs.

 

Why would you want to do this?

Creating your own MCP server lets you extend Joule Studio with custom capabilities tailored to your business processes. You can connect to SAP systems, internal APIs, or any data source to give Joule new skills that understand your specific business context.

Requirements

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry account
  • Node.js installed locally
  • Basic understanding of JavaScript/Node.js
  • Access to Joule Studio with a SAP Build developer license

 

The Development Journey: AI All the Way Down

Here's where it gets meta. I used AI tools to build a tool for AI. Let me break down the process:

Step 1: Planning with Joule for Consultants

I started by asking Joule for Consultants to help me create a development plan for building an MCP server that could be deployed to SAP BTP. Joule helped me understand the architecture requirements, the SDK I'd need, and the configuration for Joule Studio integration.

 

Step 2: Building with an AI-Powered IDE

I took the plan from Joule and used it as the starting prompt in Cline, an AI coding assistant powered by models from SAP AI Core. The AI helped me:

  • Set up the Node.js project with the correct dependencies
  • Navigate the MCP SDK's API
  • Configure the streamable HTTP transport that Joule Studio requires
  • Discover the schemas necessary for proper argument handling

Step 3: Iterative Development

The AI didn't get everything right on the first try and that's okay! This is exactly why we are starting with a proof of concept scope. Through iterative debugging, we discovered important details like:

  • The MCP SDK requires a different schema than the plan expected
  • Session management is handled automatically via the mcp-session-id header
  • The transport must be "streamable HTTP" for Joule Studio compatibility

This is the beauty of AI-assisted development: rapid iteration with an intelligent partner. If I was doing this manually I would do the same thing, I would test what I built and address the errors I get in the logs, but with a coding assistant that work is done for me and I can focus on the outcome.

 

The Architecture

The end-to-end flow looks like this:

Joule for Consultants → AI-Powered IDE → MCP Server → BTP Cloud Foundry → Joule Studio

Key Components:

  • Runtime: BTP Cloud Foundry
  • MCP SDK: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
  • Transport: Streamable HTTP (required for Joule Studio)
  • BTP Services: Cloud Foundry runtime, Destinations service

 

Building the MCP Server

Let me share the key pieces of the implementation.

Note: The code snippets below are excerpts to illustrate the key patterns. This blog is intended to share my experience building an MCP server on BTP, not serve as a step-by-step implementation guide.

Project Dependencies

{
  "name": "mcp-echo-server",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node server.js"
  },
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk": "^1.26.0",
    "express": "^5.2.1"
  }
}

 

The Echo Tool

Here's the core pattern for registering a tool with the MCP SDK:

import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
import { z } from "zod";

// Define the input schema using Zod
const echoInputSchema = z.object({
  message: z.string().describe("Input message to process")
});

// Register the tool
server.tool(
  "echo",
  "Returns 'bar' when input is 'foo'. Use this to test MCP connectivity.",
  echoInputSchema.shape,
  async (args, extra) => {
    const message = args.message;

    if (message === "foo") {
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text", text: "bar" }]
      };
    }

    return {
      content: [{ type: "text", text: `Echo: ${message}` }]
    };
  }
);

Key Learning: The MCP SDK requires Zod schemas for proper argument handling. Plain JSON Schema objects won't work—a detail we discovered through debugging!

 

Cloud Foundry Manifest

---
applications:
  - name: mcp-echo-server
    memory: 512M
    disk_quota: 1G
    instances: 1
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    random-route: true
    env:
      NODE_ENV: production
    health-check-type: http
    health-check-http-endpoint: /health

I've set memory to 512MB which is larger than needed for this simple echo server, but I'm using this as a MVP that I plan to expand. Starting with a reasonable production baseline avoids having to reconfigure everything later.

 

Deploying to SAP BTP

Deploying via BTP Cockpit

While you can use CF CLI I chose to do the deployment manually with the BTP Cockpit:

  1. Navigate to Cloud Foundry → Spaces → Your Space
  2. Click Deploy Application
  3. Upload your ZIP file
  4. Select manifest.yml for configuration
  5. Deploy and wait for the application to start

 

Configuring the Destination

This is critical for Joule Studio integration. Create an HTTP destination with these settings:

Property Value

Namemcp-echo-server
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://mcp-echo-server-<random>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
AuthenticationNoAuthentication
Additional Propertysap-joule-studio-mcp-server = true

Important: The URL must NOT end with /mcp—Joule Studio adds that automatically! And be sure to set the additional properties so it is visible from Joule Studio.

 

Integrating with Joule Studio

With the server deployed and destination configured:

  1. Open Joule Studio
  2. Navigate to MCP Servers
  3. Click Add MCP Server
  4. Select your destination (mcp-echo-server)
  5. Configure:
    • Name: Echo Server
    • Path: /mcp (default)
  6. Save

Now test it! Ask Joule to use your echo tool by sending "foo"—you should get "bar" back.

 

One of the features that is really neat is the ability to see the execution log so you can see the steps that the agent is taking. This is a great way to help debug if you need to provide more context in your instructions. It is very important to provide the agent with the context it needs to truly be an agent and make decisions based on the tools or Joule skills it has been provided.

 

Key Learnings & Tips

Here are the most important things I learned during this project:

  1. Zod is required: The MCP SDK v1.26+ requires Zod schemas for tool input validation. Plain JSON Schema objects will fail at runtime.

  2. Transport matters: Joule Studio requires streamable HTTP transport. SSE-type destinations are not supported.

  3. URL formatting: Your destination URL must NOT end with /mcp. Joule Studio appends this automatically.

  4. Session management: The SDK handles sessions automatically via the mcp-session-id header—you don't need to implement this yourself.

  5. Structured logging: Use JSON-formatted logs for easier debugging in BTP. The Application Logging service will thank you.

 

What's Next?

This echo server is just the beginning. You can extend this pattern to:

  • Query SAP S/4HANA data and expose it as a Joule skill
  • Connect to HANA databases for real-time analytics
  • Trigger SAP Build Process Automation workflows from natural language
  • Integrate with SuccessFactors for HR-related agent capabilities

 

Troubleshooting

If you encounter issues:

  • Server won't start: Check the Cloud Foundry logs in BTP Cockpit
  • Joule can't connect: Verify the destination URL doesn't end with /mcp
  • Tool not showing in Joule: Confirm the sap-joule-studio-mcp-server property is set to true
  • Schema validation errors: Ensure you're using Zod schemas, not plain JSON Schema

 

Conclusion

Using AI (from SAP AI Core) to build a tool for AI (Joule Studio), deployed on AI-ready infrastructure (BTP Cloud Foundry)—it's BTP all the way down! The barrier to entry for building MCP servers is lower than ever, especially with AI-assisted development tools at your fingertips.

 

Have you built an MCP server for Joule Studio? Please comment below if you have any questions or want to share your experience.

 

Have a great day and happy agent building!

-Max McPhee

Resources

  • SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers
  • Model Context Protocol Specification
  • Joule Studio Documentation
  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Documentation

entry.title: How to automate or simplify the mass creation of local dependencies in KMAT BOMs?
entry.content:
Hello everyone,
I am currently working in SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition) with configurable materials (KMAT) inside a Bill of Materials (BOM). The process I need to perform for each KMAT is extremely manual and error‑prone.

The current steps are:

  • Open the

 

  • Select each item

 

  • Delete the existing dependency

 

  • Create a new Local Dependency (Selection Condition)

As you can see, the process has been repeated more than 169 times. Now I need to create a local dependency; before, the dependency was global. Do you know how to streamline this process?

The process works, but it is slow, repetitive, and increases the risk of human errors.

Is there any way to automate or simplify the creation and assignment of Local Dependencies in a KMAT BOM?

If anyone has best practices, recommendations, or examples of how this is typically optimized, it would be very helpful.

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: A New System-Based ABAP Certification Is Here - What Developers Need to Know
entry.content:
🚀SAP Certified - Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud (Beta Version)

 If you’re planning to take it, here’s what you should know - and how to prepare effectively.

💡Good to know!

The new exam https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-back-end-developer-abap-cloud focuses on solving practical development tasks based on the content of:

  • Basic ABAP Programming (S4D400)
  • Intermediate ABAP Programming (S4D401)
  • Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services (S4D430)

You’ll work in an ABAP environment on SAP BTP, using the ABAP language version for cloud development.

If your background is primarily on-premise ABAP, this is a mindset shift.

 ℹ️We’re currently in the Beta phase of the certification exam.

What that means for you:

  •  3-hour exam instead of the planned 4-hour version
  • Same full certification as the final release - no “beta” label
  • Early opportunity before the scope expands

 

After Beta, the exam will be extended to 4 hours with broader coverage.

Don’t work harder, work smarter. Secure your spot now!

 

The Environment – ABAP Cloud

ABAP Cloud promotes:

  • Model-driven development
  • Use of Core Data Services (CDS)
  • ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP)
  • Automatic exposure of services (OData/Web APIs)
    All leading to faster, more scalable apps and services. 

🤓Hint: Before you start, be sure to update your Eclipse and ADT installations to the latest versions.

 

Why Practice Systems Are Critical

The certification system will resemble the training and practice systems available in learning environments - but it won’t be identical.

 The practice systems, including the exercise handbooks and system setup guides to practice and prepare, can be found here:

  • Basic ABAP Programming S4D400
  • Intermediate ABAP Programming S4D401
  • Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services S4D430

🤓Hint: Please refer to the course file that is downloaded when clicking the access button for further log on information

 

 Preparation is Essential.

 The practice systems give you access to:

  • The same underlying data model used in the exam
  • Identical table and field names
  • A comparable development setup

 Getting familiar with the structure of the data model and working with ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse (ADT) will save you valuable time during the exam.

 ⏲ And time matters!

You’ll have three hours to complete the exam.  Three hours go faster than you think!

🤓Hint: Joule for Developers is available within the exam system. We recommend using it while working through the tasks to help you complete them more efficiently.

 

 How to Prepare

Along with the practice Systems mentioned above, the Learning Journey  Acquiring Core ABAP Skills will help you to prepare for your exam.

 

Good Luck 🍀 and Happy Learning!

 

For more information on the Reimagine Certification initiative, please read: Reimagining Certification for the Way We Work Toda... - SAP Community

 

SAP Learning Services Topic Page 
Ask questions about Training and follow 
Read other Training blog posts and follow

entry.title: Re: ZLOCAL is not a valid software component for package...
entry.content:
From the information that you are using an Eclipse 2022 that is outdated for the use of SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud for more than 3 years I conclude that you are working on an on-premise or private cloud system and not on an SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud System.

Therefore you have used the wrong tag

SAP S/4HANA Cloud ABAP Environment

In an SAP S/4HANA 2022 there is indeed no software component ZLOCAL available and you will not be able to run any tutorial that is related to the ABAP Environment of SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

How to set up Developer Extensibility with software components such as ZLOCAL and ZCUSTOM_DEVELOPMENT in SAP S/4HANA on prem or private cloud system (as of 2023) is described here:

https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_NEW/b5670aaaa2364a29935f40b16499972d/31367ef6c3e947059e0d7c1cbfcaae93.html?version=LATEST&locale=en-US

Can you please add the link of the tutorial you are following to your question and can you please change the tag to "ABAP Development" if you are not using an ABAP Environment of SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?

Kind regards,

Andre

entry.title: Re: DMS document configuration in SAP Service and Asset M...
entry.content:
It sounds like you're encountering an issue with integrating OpenText DMS documents into the SSAM application. As you've noted, the BDS object is available, but the DMS object seems to be missing from the SSAM frontend code. It's possible that the standard SSAM 2310 configuration doesn't include DMS support out of the box, which might explain the absence of the DMS object in the frontend.

To resolve this, I recommend checking the system's configuration settings and ensuring that the DMS integration is enabled. You may also want to verify that the correct plugins or modules for DMS are installed. If the default setup doesn’t support DMS, you might need to implement custom configurations or use additional connectors to enable DMS functionality.

If you're looking for a reliable solution for document management, you could explore platforms like ATLRx.com , which offers seamless integration options for various document and content management systems.

Hi,

I'm currently upgrading my local environment to the latest Sap Commerce version of jdk 21, IEP 8, I think I managed to make all the required changes stated in the various guides https://me.sap.com/notes/3653999/E but I receive one last error while starting my server:1

[TenantIgnoreXmlWebApplicationContext] Exception encountered during context initialization - cancelling refresh attempt: org.springframework.beans.factory.BeanDefinitionStoreException: Invalid bean definition with name 'addonCMSComponentRendererRegistry' defined in class path resource [addonsupport/web/spring/addonsupport-web-spring.xml]: Could not resolve parent bean definition 'acceleratorCMSComponentRendererRegistry'
ERROR [Catalina-utility-2] [ContextLoader] Context initialization failed
org.springframework.beans.factory.BeanDefinitionStoreException: Invalid bean definition with name 'addonCMSComponentRendererRegistry' defined in class path resource [addonsupport/web/spring/addonsupport-web-spring.xml]: Could not resolve parent bean definition 'acceleratorCMSComponentRendererRegistry'

As far as I know we never made changes to the default bean declaration (spring-cms-config.xml) that was in our yacceleratorstorefront when we extended it, our beans are declared as follows and they were working properly with the previous release:

<!-- CMS Component Renderers -->

	<alias name="defaultCMSLinkComponentRenderer" alias="cmsLinkComponentRenderer"/>
	<bean id="defaultCMSLinkComponentRenderer" class="org.ducati.storefront.renderer.CMSLinkComponentRenderer" >
		<property name="categoryUrlConverter" ref="categoryUrlConverter"/>
		<property name="productUrlConverter" ref="productUrlConverter"/>
	</bean>

	<alias name="defaultImageMapComponentRenderer" alias="imageMapComponentRenderer"/>
	<bean id="defaultImageMapComponentRenderer" class="org.ducati.storefront.renderer.ImageMapComponentRenderer" />


	<alias alias="cmsComponentRendererRegistry" name="acceleratorCMSComponentRendererRegistry" />
	<bean id="acceleratorCMSComponentRendererRegistry" parent="defaultCMSComponentRendererRegistry">
		<property name="renderers">
			<map merge="true">
				<entry key="CMSLinkComponent" value-ref="cmsLinkComponentRenderer"/>
				<entry key="ImageMapComponent" value-ref="imageMapComponentRenderer"/>
			</map>
		</property>
	</bean>

 

Did someone have experienced the same issue? How can I be sure I didn't mess up with some beans declaration?

Thank you for your support

entry.title: Re: Is Subscription Mandatory for completing SAP certific...
entry.content:
Based on SAP's official guidelines, here is the answers to your questions regarding SAP certification:

  • Subscription is not mandatory to take the initial SAP certification exam; you can purchase standalone exam attempts (e.g., single attempt for 40k-50k INR) .

  • However, an SAP Learning Hub subscription is required to complete the annual "stay certified" assessment to maintain your credential without re-taking the full exam .

Steps for SAP Certification:

  • First, choose your certification on the SAP Learning site and complete the corresponding "Learning Journey" .

  • Second, purchase either a subscription or a standalone exam attempt via the SAP Certification Hub .

  • Finally, schedule and take the exam, which as of 2026 is transitioning to a practical, scenario-based format

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay - Eligibility Rules
entry.content:
@SOFIAANNA_VAMVOUKLI Yes we would need more details as per @lesleyr 's comment. 

However, based on what is written in your question I would say none of these 2 rules is what directly impacts which records are going to be retrieved in Employee History.

Both of these rules are applied on records already in Emp History.

If what you are asking about is how to make sure you get only the right records with the right dates in Employee history then you need to work only with these two :

  • Global Eligibility Rule
  • Eligibility Rule for Employee history Records

 

If you have no idea how these rules work, here is a breakdown:

--> Both rules are INCLUSION rules that eventually impact ONLY EMPLOYEE HISTORY RECORDS (but if you know Variable pay you also know that if there is no record in employee history for someone then the person doesn't get added to a worksheet when you launch them).

 

EXAMPLE

➡ Imagine your sibling and their 4 kids come over to your house for lunch.
➡ You ask the kids if they want cake, to which your sibling says "I'll tell you what they can have". 

--> Your sibling is your customer giving you eligibility requirements for variable pay eligibility in workshops and the kids are their employees.

 


➡ Your sibling says "Only kids older than 5 can have cake" (2 out of 4 kids are older than 5 yo). To build this you are first going to use the Global Eligibility rule that allows to trim the employee history to only make it run on specific USERIDs. So you're going to make a rule of type variable pay eligibility (in Configure Business Rules) that simply says "Age > 5 years old". (In a way this rule is similar to the compensation inclusion rule when you start from nobody is eligible except that here it is intricately connected with the other rule below on records as you will see in the next paragraph). 

  • Instead of the 5 years old example a real business example would be : only allows records to be retrieved for employees that are still ACTIVE or on PAID LEAVE as of March 1st (bonus cycle start date). Meaning only employees still with the company on March 1st will get a payout for the time they spent in an eligible position between 1/1 and 12/31 of previous year.
  • This rule is critical when you are building VarPay as I explain in recording #1 of this blog : https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/3-must-know-configuration-setups-in-variable-pay/ba-p/13514367 

 


➡ Your sibling now says "Kids that finished their homework can have one slice, those who didn't can only have half a slice" : here you Record eligibility rule to scan among kids eligible based on the Global eligible rule above (older than 5) whether they finished their homework. Note that this rules only runs between 1/1 and 12/31 (or any other program dates you defined in Plan Setup > Settings > Bonus Start Date & Bonus End Date).

  • In employee history once you run the job it would give you something like this:
    • Kid 1 (finished their homework) --> one record with start date = 1/1 and end date = 12/31
    • Kid 2 (didn't finish their homework) --> one record with start date = 1/1 and end date = 6/1 (50%).

A real business example here would be: only active records should be retrieved which means that if an employee was in an UNPAID leave of absence between March 2nd and May 2nd it would get prorated out and we would hence get the following once we run the rule :

  • 1/1 to 3/1 
  • 5/1 to 12/31

 

Hope this helps.

Best, 

Xavier

entry.title: Understanding how to retrieve only the right employee records in Variable Pay
entry.content:
Introduction

One of the hardest things to understand for consultants working with SAP SuccessFactors Variable Pay is how to build customer eligibility rules which means how to retrieve only the right records to make sure that only the right people are showing and that proration is done correctly for everybody.

It's something so complex that sometimes even the most experienced consultants need to take many steps backs and either request new fields added to EC or build their own MDF with Integration Center as a workaround to accurately capture some customer requirements (see this blog or this blog).

 

Visual example (from an implementation partner standpoint)

➡ Imagine your sibling and their 4 kids come over to your house for lunch.
➡ You ask the kids if they want cake, to which your sibling says "I will tell you what they can have". 

➡ Your sibling is your customer giving you eligibility requirements for variable pay eligibility in workshops and the kids are their employees.

 

 


➡ First, your sibling says "Only kids older than 5 can have cake" (2 out of 4 kids are older than 5 yo). To build this you are first going to use the Global Eligibility rule that allows you to trim the employee history to only make it run on specific USERIDs. So you're going to create a business rule of type variable pay eligibility that simply says "Age > 5 years old". (In a way this rule is similar to the salary review templates inclusion rule when you start from nobody is eligible except that here it is intricately connected with the other rule below as you will see in the next paragraph). 

 

  • A real business example here would be : only retrieve records for employees that are still ACTIVE or on PAID LEAVE as of March 1st (bonus cycle start date). Meaning only employees still with the company on March 1st will get a payout for the time they spent in an eligible position between 1/1 and 12/31 of previous year.
  • This rule is critical when you are configuring Variable Pay as I've explained in recording #1 of this blog (knowing how to use it can save you entire days of work) : https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/3-must-know-configuration-se... 

 

 

 


➡ Second, your sibling says "Kids that finished their homework can have one slice, those who didn't can only have half a slice" : here you are going to use the Record eligibility rule to look at eligible kids school books and confirm whether they actually finished their homework and retrieve a record in employee history accordingly. Note that you don't need to add the Age condition as it has already been captured by the Global Eligibility rule as explained in the previous paragraph. Also note that this rules only runs between 1/1 and 12/31 (or any other program dates you defined in Plan Setup > Settings > Bonus Start Date & Bonus End Date).

 

  • In employee history once you run the "import employee history from EC" job it would give you something like this:
    • Kid 1 (finished their homework) --> one record with start date = 1/1 and end date = 12/31
    • Kid 2 (didn't finish their homework) --> one record with start date = 1/1 and end date = 6/1 (proration of bonus payout is handled through dates of records retrieved in employee history - see excel from this blog).

A real business example here would be: only active records should be retrieved which means that if an employee was in an UNPAID leave of absence between March 2nd and May 2nd it would get prorated out and we would hence get the following once we run the import employee history job :

  • 1/1 to 3/1 
  • 5/1 to 12/31

 

 

 

In a workshop you can use the real business examples I listed above to drive discussions and it is usually enough. However you may want to look at the complex requirements listed as links in the introduction and make sure there are no requirements like this.

Once you have the right records in employee history, they must be tied to a Bonus Plan for an employee to show on a worksheet. For this see this blog.

 

Best, 

Xavier

 

--


If you found this blog useful please consider giving it a kudo !

entry.title: Re: IaC for SAP BTP: Automating Environment Management with Terraform and CI/CD
entry.content:
@lechnerc77 Thanks for sharing your perspective. 🙂

Of course, there are many other options for managing Terraform state, including native services like AWS S3 or Azure Blob Storage, as well as platforms such as Terraform Cloud.

The main goal of the article was to demonstrate how components available within SAP BTP can be used as part of an Infrastructure as Code workflow and to encourage BTP Cloud Engineers to leverage IaC practices when managing BTP environments. The Object Store example was simply meant to illustrate one possible setup within the BTP ecosystem.

In larger organizations especially, the final choice usually depends on the existing tool landscape, DevOps practices, and governance requirements. For that reason, the example in the article should be seen as just one possible approach rather than a strict recommendation.

Thanks again for raising the point.

entry.title: Re: Catalog Profile in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition...
entry.content:
Dear @9150,

How it works in Public Cloud:

  • You can restrict which code groups (catalog groups) appear in maintenance notifications / requests by controlling what’s included in a Catalog Profile
  • In Public Cloud this is done via Fiori apps not classic IMG Define Catalog Profile customizing
    • Create code groups/codes in Edit Code Group
    • Then add those code groups to a catalog profile in Maintain Catalog Profile
  • You then assign the catalog profile to the technical object (Equipment / Functional Location). SAP even gives the navigation example in the technical object change app
  • SAP notes a common gotcha the change is available for newly created notifications existing ones may not be impacted
  • If dropdowns are empty ensure the assigned Catalog Profile contains the required catalogs for example Catalog D Coding) / or use the standard profile (YB-PM0001)

SAP Notes below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3659603

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3563199

IMPORTANT:

If you don’t see the apps Maintain Catalog Profile / Edit Code Groups in your tenant, usually it means the app isn’t in your Fiori launchpad due to role/content, not that the capability doesn’t exist. The solution is to ensure the user role includes access to Maintain Catalog Profile and Edit Code Group apps

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('OQN6_MD')/S36

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('QS41')/S36

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Poland einvoicing Customer Reference to NrZamowienia field #COE #SW01
entry.content:
S4Hana Public Cloud 2SL. In Billing documents the Customer Reference (billing_doc_header-bstnk_vf) contains the PO number. It is mapped to the field fa-warunki_transakcji-NrZamowienia . However, it only shows on the einvoice XML when a billing document is created from a Sales Order.

We are creating Billing documents using the BDR Excel upload template. However, the Customer Reference field is not passed to NrZamowienia.
I tried using custom logic  EDOC_PL_KSEF_XML_INVOICE, but it does not work. Does anyone have code that can make it work?

DATA: lv_output TYPE string,
ls_invoice_details TYPE edo_pl_faktura_fa,
ls_zamowienie TYPE LINE OF edo_pl_faktura_fa-WARUNKI_TRANSAKCJI-ZAMOWIENIA.

xml_file_modified = xml_file.
ls_invoice_details = xml_file_modified-fa.

" Fill Zamowienia
CLEAR ls_zamowienie.
ls_zamowienie-SEQUENCE-nr_zamowienia = source_data-billing_doc_header-bstnk_vf.
APPEND ls_zamowienie TO ls_invoice_details-warunki_transakcji-zamowienia.

" Assign back
xml_file_modified-fa = ls_invoice_details.

entry.title: Re: Audit Logs Made Simple: Instant Access to the SAP Audit Log Viewer in SAP BTP
entry.content:
I'm sorry David but that's incorrect. When visiting the Audit Log Viewer Instance at global account level, it will -only- present you with the opportunity to login with S-user. It was even confirmed above by evasiarri when reported previously by GabiMaier.

So while I'm happy it was rolled out, it definitely needs to support S-user to be a viable option for many. As that's literally the first security recommendation  for setting up BTP.

entry.title: Re: Error ' Essential transfer parameters are missing in...
entry.content:
Dear @kkumara,

In Stage Material for Production Order (MF60) this happens when the system tries to create the delivery-based replenishment element (delivery) from the EWM-managed storage location to the production destination but mandatory shipping data is missing most commonly shipping point, sometimes also goods recipient depending on your setup

SAP Notes below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3408921

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3067316

MF60 staging uses delivery processing. For the delivery, SAP must determine shipping data (incl. shipping point). If it cannot, it raises VL561 and no staging delivery is created

Recommended steps:

Step 1:

In CBC:

  • Search for 180019 Define Shipping Data for Stock Transfers Between Storage Locations

     

  • Maintain the entry for your Plant + issuing (EWM) storage location and the relevant receiving storage location if required by the activity and fill the Shipping Point and any other required fields shown in the activity

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3408921

Step 2:

Go back to MF60 and execute staging again. If a replenishment element/delivery was created but stuck, reprocess it after the customizing is maintained

Additional information:

If VL561 still appears can also be triggered if Goods Recipient is missing in the delivery-relevant data. In similar EWM-integrated delivery creation scenarios, SAP documents that maintaining the Goods Recipient resolves the same VL561 symptom

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3639161

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: RISE Private Cloud – On-prem subnet unreachable after ISP failover
entry.content:
We are running SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud (RISE on AWS) connected to on-premise via Direct Connect with dual ISP backup.

During primary ISP failure, connectivity to a specific on-premise subnet is lost while other subnets remain reachable.

This suggests a potential routing asymmetry or selective route propagation issue during failover.

We would like to validate routing symmetry and reachability from the SAP cloud side.

Questions:

  • Is it possible to execute traceroute or TCP connectivity tests from SAP managed application servers in RISE environments?
  • Is this typically handled by SAP Cloud Operations via incident ticket?
  • Are there recommended validation procedures from SAP side during customer ISP failover scenarios?
